data.definition: Versioned RevDeck extension package that declares capabilities, permissions, schemas, compatibility, commands, and lifecycle hooks.
Declared plugin role such as Importer, Analyzer, Lens, Scorer, Exporter, Action, Rule Pack, or Adapter.
Recorded execution of a built-in analyzer or plugin, including inputs, outputs, diagnostics, version, timing, and provenance.
Ordered set of RevDeck objects, edges, notes, traces, diffs, or external references supporting a finding.

data.title: No exploit automation positioning
data.rationale: RevDeck should support authorized reverse engineering and evidence workflows, not exploitation, malware deployment, credential theft, or bypass automation as a product promise.

data.title: No direct private database mutation through SDK
data.rationale: Plugin authors should use stable object graph APIs so RevDeck can preserve provenance, migrations, integrity, and compatibility.

data.description: Machine-validated plugin manifest with ID, version, compatibility, capabilities, permissions, schemas, commands, lens registrations, and provenance metadata.
SDK types and APIs for normalized objects, edges, artifacts, analysis runs, evidence links, diagnostics, and findings.
Least-privilege plugin execution model covering filesystem, network, external processes, write scopes, resource limits, redaction, and audit trails.
Plugins can propose draft findings, evidence chains, report sections, and exports while analyst confirmation remains explicit for final deliverables.

data.insight: RevDeck should make manifest plus schema-validated process protocol the v0.9 stable boundary, avoiding Rust dynamic-library ABI promises while keeping plugins language-neutral.
Plugin output should be buffered as validated graph deltas and committed by the host through revdeck-db repositories under one analysis_run, not written directly to SQLite by plugins.
Every run should store a digest over plugin version, schema version, config, artifact refs, selected project objects, and accepted output event sequence to support replay and mismatch detection.
Permissions should be first-class grants scoped by artifact read, project read, project write, filesystem, network, process spawning, and secrets, with network and secrets denied by default.
Trace, crash, protocol, firmware, memory, diff, and graph labs should begin as declarative lens/query/inspector contributions over core object families before custom rendering or plugin-owned tables.

positions: System architecture favors core-owned object families plus metadata extensions for v0.9 safety.
Product and domain roles may want rapid custom object types for specialized labs and adapters.
data.resolution: [SUGGESTED] Allow metadata extensions first; graduate recurring plugin object shapes into core migrations after schema, query, and TUI rendering rules are defined.

data.insight: The SDK MVP should be marketed as a local plugin SDK preview: manifest, stable schema, adapters, permissions, and minimal test harness before marketplace or broad custom lenses.
Importer and adapter plugins deliver the quickest user value because they turn existing Ghidra/rizin, Frida, ASAN, binwalk, and tshark outputs into navigable RevDeck evidence.
Trace Lab and Crash Lab should lead specialized extension points because they create clear evidence chains and triage queues; Firmware Lab follows for multi-artifact workspace expansion.
Plugin-created findings should progress through suggestion, draft, and analyst-confirmed states so reports do not blur automated output with human conclusions.
Core should keep Binary Triage useful, Pro can package first-party adapter packs and advanced labs, and Team can monetize private registries, signing policy, audit export, and shared rule packs.

positions: Marketplace has ecosystem and packaging upside.
Safety, compatibility, signing, deterministic tests, and trust metadata must exist first.
data.resolution: Defer public marketplace; ship local plugins, first-party examples, and curated/private distribution before public plugin discovery.

positions: Specialized labs are compelling user-facing roadmap items.
Broad lens APIs can fragment UX and data contracts before schemas mature.
data.resolution: Treat custom lenses as constrained/experimental while prioritizing schema-backed adapters, common navigation, Inspector reuse, notes, tags, and findings integration.

data.insight: RevDeck should prioritize adapters for Ghidra/radare2/rizin, Frida/QEMU traces, ASAN logs, binwalk trees, tshark, and Volatility before native reimplementation of specialized analysis engines.
Static addresses, runtime PCs, firmware offsets, crash frame PCs, protocol byte ranges, and memory virtual addresses must be modeled as distinct address spaces with explicit rebasing metadata.
Specialized labs should expose confidence for inferred functions, protocol fields, crash signatures, suspicious memory regions, and source-to-sink paths so heuristics do not appear analyst-confirmed.
The highest-value workflows link firmware routes, strings, functions, trace events, crash frames, protocol messages, memory objects, diffs, notes, and findings into one navigable evidence chain.

positions: SME workflows need enough domain objects for traces, crashes, firmware, protocol, and memory to avoid lossy adapters.
Architecture favors core-owned families plus metadata extensions before arbitrary plugin object types.
data.resolution: [SUGGESTED] Define a minimal core domain object set for common RE evidence and allow typed metadata extensions; defer arbitrary plugin-owned object families until query, migration, and rendering contracts mature.

data.insight: Plugin findings and reports should default to redacting credential candidates, private keys, packet payloads, and memory strings, with audited analyst choice for unredacted export.
RevDeck should keep core object kinds host-owned for v0.9 and let plugins extend them through schema-registered typed attributes before allowing custom object kinds.
Plugin SDK writes should be ObjectBatch transactions validated by host APIs; direct SQLite writes should remain unsupported so core schema can evolve safely.
source_run_id is useful but insufficient for multi-plugin enrichment; RevDeck needs contribution records for objects, edges, attributes, scores, and findings.
High-volume traces, packets, memory maps, and fuzz cases should use host-managed extension datasets and typed cursors rather than arbitrary plugin-owned tables.

positions: Arbitrary plugin tables maximize flexibility but weaken migrations, export, permissioning, and shared lenses.
Host-owned core schemas and declared datasets reduce plugin freedom but preserve stable query and report workflows.
data.resolution: Use staged extensibility: typed attributes and host-managed datasets in the first SDK; custom object kinds only after query, migration, and bundle compatibility tests exist.

data.insight: Plugin UI contributions should be declared as host-owned slots: workspace lenses, command actions, inspector cards, cockpit chips, triage reasons, finding suggestions, and help sections.
Command Deck should be the universal discovery layer for plugin commands, required permissions, examples, diagnostics, and context-sensitive actions added by plugins.
Specialized labs and plugin lenses should begin as declarative registry entries using constrained host templates before stable plugins can provide custom terminal renderers.
The plugin SDK should validate labels, badges, table columns, inspector cards, and help snippets against compact, standard, wide, and extra-wide terminal breakpoints.
Permission and execution safety states should appear as cockpit chips, blocked lens states, and Command Deck grant summaries rather than only runtime errors.

positions: Plugin authors may want global shortcuts and branded UI for visibility.
RevDeck needs stable keyboard muscle memory, consistent safety colors, and predictable terminal behavior.
data.resolution: Host owns global shortcuts, focus behavior, and state colors; plugins can suggest aliases, labels, help examples, and opt-in shortcut bindings.

data.insight: The plugin SDK should ship with a public conformance suite covering manifest validation, host/plugin protocol events, graph-delta validation, fixture replay, sandbox denial, deterministic output, and CLI/TUI regression.
Golden tests should compare normalized graph bundles, accepted event digest sequences, diagnostics, and query outputs rather than raw SQLite tables or brittle terminal screenshots.

P0 sandbox tests need fake plugins that attempt denied filesystem, network, process, environment, DB, timeout, output-limit, and malformed-protocol behavior; expected failures must be recorded, sanitized, and non-mutating.

Deterministic plugins should emit the same accepted event digest sequence for identical package, manifest, input, config, project schema, and SDK version; reruns must be idempotent and avoid duplicate objects or edges.

Plugin UI testing should stay reducer-first with ratatui TestBackend sizes for small and large terminals, covering command registration, diagnostics, inspector sections, draft finding labels, and lens navigation.

positions: Broad plugin APIs and marketplace distribution accelerate ecosystem demos.
Sandbox, compatibility, deterministic replay, and conformance gates are needed before users can trust plugins with samples, memory dumps, credentials, and private notes.
data.resolution: [SUGGESTED] Ship local SDK preview with first-party proof plugins and conformance tests first; defer public marketplace until safety and compatibility gates pass.

data.insight: M1 should combine manifest validation, stable ObjectBatch schemas, plugin execution safety, and minimal conformance tooling before any marketplace or broad custom UI extension work.
RevDeck becomes versatile fastest by importing external-tool outputs into a unified evidence graph, not by implementing every specialized analyzer in core.

positions: Marketplace can drive ecosystem growth.
Trust, permissions, deterministic replay, signing, and compatibility gates are prerequisites.
data.resolution: [RESOLVED] Defer public marketplace; ship local SDK preview plus first-party proof plugins first.

positions: Custom renderers make plugins visually powerful.
Host-owned templates preserve keyboard model, responsive behavior, safety colors, and testability.
data.resolution: [SUGGESTED] Use declarative lens templates first; custom renderers remain experimental until templates prove insufficient.

data.insight: Cross-role synthesis supports a staged roadmap: schema and manifest lock, local SDK preview, adapter proof pack, workflow depth, governed distribution, then marketplace evaluation.
The first marketable SDK should combine F-001, F-002, F-006, and a thin F-007 so plugin authors can validate, run, replay, and test local plugins before public distribution exists.
A credible proof pack should cover at least one static export adapter, one trace importer, one crash importer, one firmware-tree importer, and one explainable scoring pack.
Typed attributes and host-managed datasets satisfy most early adapter and lab needs while preserving queryability, migration, export, permissions, and TUI consistency.
Plugin-driven findings should use suggestion and draft states until an analyst explicitly confirms final report content, preserving provenance and report credibility.

positions: Marketplace distribution improves ecosystem visibility.
Safety, trust, compatibility, permission UX, deterministic replay, and responsible-use review must be proven first.
data.resolution: [RESOLVED] Defer public marketplace; ship local SDK preview, first-party proof plugins, conformance suite, signing/trust metadata, and private distribution pattern first.

positions: Custom kinds and raw tables maximize plugin flexibility.
Host-owned schemas, typed attributes, and extension datasets preserve queryability, migration, export, and testability.
data.resolution: [RESOLVED] Keep v0.9 stable SDK to core object families, typed attributes, and host-managed datasets; revisit custom kinds after compatibility tests exist.

positions: Plugin authors may want visual control, branded renderers, and global shortcuts.
RevDeck needs consistent keyboard behavior, safety colors, accessibility, and regression-testable terminal layouts.
data.resolution: [SUGGESTED] Use declarative lens slots and host templates for stable plugins; custom renderers and shortcuts stay experimental or opt-in until snapshot and compatibility tests mature.
